questions.question: Which searching algorithm requires the array to be sorted before it can be used?
questions.explanation: Binary search requires the array to be sorted because it repeatedly divides the search interval in half, comparing the target to the middle element.

questions.question: What is the worst-case number of comparisons for a linear search on an array of 1000 elements?
questions.explanation: Linear search checks each element one by one. In the worst case (element not found or at the last position), it must check all 1000 elements.

questions.question: In bubble sort, after the first complete pass through an array of N elements, which element is guaranteed to be in its correct position?
questions.explanation: Bubble sort repeatedly swaps adjacent elements if they are in the wrong order. After the first pass, the largest value has 'bubbled up' to the last position.

questions.question: How does selection sort find the value to place in position 0 of the array?
choices: It swaps adjacent elements until the smallest reaches position 0
It scans the entire array to find the smallest value and swaps it into position 0
It inserts each element into its correct position relative to sorted elements
It divides the array in half and sorts each half
questions.explanation: Selection sort scans the entire unsorted portion to find the minimum value, then swaps it into the next position in the sorted portion, starting with position 0.

questions.question: A ___ search steps through an array sequentially, comparing each element to the target value.
questions.explanation: A linear search (also called sequential search) examines each element in order from the first to the last until the target is found or the array is exhausted.

questions.question: The maximum number of comparisons a binary search performs on a sorted array of N elements is approximately log2(___)
questions.explanation: Binary search has a worst-case complexity of O(log2 N) because it halves the search space with each comparison.
